feedback.text: Hello Good, I would like to comment on the initiative that many EU citizens share: The fact that we try to improve protection for users of digital products seems to me to be fantastic. Many consumer rights have been violated for years and it was time to do something. For my part, dark patterns and loot boxes seem to me to be a clear door to ludopathy and open the doors to which many people may be affected, especially children. This does not mean that access to digital entertainment must be permanently restricted for minors, or that users’ privacy must be violated. In these cases, I suggest applying more education and awareness-raising measures. Also, as an alternative a bit more restrictive, it could only be forced to authenticate with your data as a user at the time of a purchase and to ban free loot boxes to try to convince the user to buy one. I personally believe that it would be the most effective way of limiting this type of dark pattern without affecting the anonymity or freedom of users. Finally, I would like to add, it would also be very beneficial to protect access to your products once purchased. I therefore focus on all those companies which do not allow the use of an earlier version of a previously obtained programme, offering only the last version as an alternative. It also applies to payment services or products that, after the end of the medium, block your access or force you to delete or destroy your copy. As consumers, we have the right to access all the goods and services for which we have paid. It is a fundamental right that is constantly violated in a market that is only concerned about maximising benefits at the expense of consumers. I understand that it is a little free to come here to propose to amend the initiative to include a completely new paragraph, but it is of paramount importance that we protect our rights, and ownership of copies (whether of software or hardware) is a very important and critical point to consider. We only want that if we end up supporting a service, product or game for which we have paid, it can still be used locally. Or at least the code is released so that it can be used by those who bought it. It may appear, especially after seeing more comments than usual, that we are only a few fanatical users trying to be heard with their complaints and demands... But the truth is that, in reality, we are only a small proportion of the countless European consumers and users who have seen their rights violated over the last 15 years in digital and digital property rights. The industry has reached a hotspot, and we can no longer look further. This is something we all have in common. We kindly ask you to seriously consider the implications of not protecting digital property rights in the long term and the changing trends experienced by the technology industry in the last decades. Thank you for reading a humble citizen and interested in our rights. I hope that the initiative can be beneficial and will lead to a healthier and fairer digital market.
